channel.title: Signals on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Signals on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] C signal() Deprecated — Replace with sigaction()
item.description:
[Solution] C signal() Deprecated — Replace with sigaction()

The signal() function is deprecated in POSIX because its behavior is unreliable and implementation-defined. When a signal handler is invoked, the handler disposition may be reset to SIG_DFL, requiring the handler to re-register itself — creating a race condition. sigaction() provides explicit control over signal behavior and is the recommended replacement.

What You’ll See

Compiler warnings with strict POSIX compliance flags:

warning: 'signal' is deprecated: use sigaction() for reliable signal handling

Why Deprecated

signal() is deprecated because:

item.title: [Solution] Linux EINTR (errno 4) — Interrupted System Call Fix
item.description:
Linux EINTR (errno 4) — Interrupted System Call

EINTR (errno 4) occurs when a blocking system call is interrupted by a signal and the signal handler returns. The kernel aborts the system call and returns this error to inform your program that the call did not complete. Commonly affected calls include read(), write(), accept(), connect(), sleep(), and wait(). This error is fundamental to Unix signal handling and must be handled in any robust application.
